title: Drying off a dairy goat
subtitle: Stopping milk production safely at the end of the lactation.
excerpt: A dairy goat in full milk that you stop milking abruptly is at risk of mastitis, the udder fills, the pressure builds, and bacteria find the teat orifice an easy entry point. Drying off correctly means reducing milk production gradually over two to three weeks, monitoring the udder, and confirming both halves have dried before you stop entirely.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Goats. SAC Consulting Scotland, Dairy Goat Management. AHDB, Mastitis control guidance (adapted for small ruminants).

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of stopping a dairy animal's milk production at the end of its lactation period, allowing the udder to rest before the next kidding.
Inflammation and infection of the udder, usually bacterial; characterised by a hot, hard, or lumpy udder half and watery or curdled milk.
A disinfectant solution applied to the teat after milking to seal the teat orifice and prevent bacterial entry.

content.text: Most dairy goats are dried off six to eight weeks before kidding to allow the udder and the doe's body to rest. For a doe in her second or later lactation, this means planning the

around eight weeks before her due date. A doe still producing three or more litres a day needs a gradual reduction before a clean stop; a doe producing under a litre a day can often be stopped over a few days.
Three weeks before the planned stop date, reduce milking from twice to once daily. This naturally drops production.
One week before the stop, milk every other day. Production should now be well below a litre daily.
On the last milking day, strip the udder completely, disinfect both teats with

For the first week after the last milking, check the udder daily. A firm, slightly full udder is normal, milk reabsorption is under way. A hot, hard, or painful udder half, or a doe grinding her teeth or refusing to walk normally, suggests
. Strip a small amount into a black cup or onto a dark surface, watery milk, visible clots, or blood-tinged discharge is abnormal. Call your vet if you find these signs.

Antibiotic dry-off tubes designed for cows can be used off-label in goats with veterinary prescription. This is most appropriate for a doe with a history of subclinical mastitis in previous lactations. Discuss with your vet whether this makes sense for your animal; routine use in healthy goats is not recommended.

Reduce concentrate feeding when production drops below a litre daily. Excess concentrate drives milk production and makes the dry-off harder. Hay and browse continue as normal. Don't restrict water, a dry doe still needs full water access for the pregnancy.
